text: The surgeon dissects the mesosigmoid and ligates the inferior mesenteric pedicle, dividing it. The pelvic dissection is performed, and the rectum is divided while the patient is in Trendelenburg position with the right side down. The surgical team is positioned with the surgeon and cameraman on the right and the first assistant on the left, operating through right-sided cannulas with monitors placed at the foot of the bed.

The surgeon mobilizes the left colon and splenic flexure, operating through the suprapubic and left lower quadrant cannulas while standing between the patient's legs. The assistant and cameraman are positioned on the patient's right, and the surgical team observes the procedure on a monitor near the patient's left shoulder.

In phase three of the colectomy, the surgeon mobilizes the right colon and hepatic flexure while positioned between the patient's legs, using the suprapubic and right lower quadrant cannulas. The assistant and cameraman are now positioned on the patient's left, with the surgical team focused on the monitor at the patient's right shoulder.

The assistant grasps the mesosigmoid and retracts it anteriorly. The surgeon incises the peritoneum to the right of the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) and continues the incision down to the sacral promontory and up to the IMA level. Blunt dissection is used to sweep the IMA anteriorly, away from the preaortic hypogastric neural plexus. The dissection proceeds medially beneath the IMA and superior rectal artery, identifying and sweeping the left ureter and gonadal vessels posteriorly. The IMA is visible to the left, with the ureter in the background.

The surgeon creates a peritoneal window lateral to the inferior mesenteric artery and vein. The vascular pedicle is draped over a grasper, and the inferior mesenteric artery and vein are ligated below the left colic artery using an endoscopic vascular stapler. The left ureter is clearly identified and retracted to avoid injury, with its position highlighted in the surgical field.

Following the ligation of the pedicle, the surgeon continues dissection in the retroperitoneum, elevating the mesosigmoid towards the lateral abdominal wall. The sigmoid colon is mobilized past the sacral promontory, and the thin lateral peritoneal attachments are dissected, completing the mobilization of the sigmoid colon.

A member of the operating team identifies the distal resection line on the rectum using a rigid proctoscope. Dissection of the mesorectum begins on the right side, with larger vessels managed using clips or an endoscopic linear stapler. The rectum is divided with two applications of a three-centimeter endoscopic stapler.

Following the division of the rectum, the assistant introduces an endoscopic snare through the right upper quadrant cannula. The surgeon uses a grasper to pull the cut end of the colon through the snare loop. The distal colon is flipped over the shaft of the snare retractor, enhancing exposure of the posterior mesenteric attachments.

The surgeon dissects in a cephalad direction, sharply dividing the lateral peritoneal attachments in the left colonic mesentery. Girota's fascia is swept away from the posterior surface of the colonic mesentery, and the base of the left mesocolon is divided as the lateral peritoneal attachments are taken down.

The surgeon uses a snare retractor to apply traction on the peritoneal attachments of the colon, moving it proximally to take down the splenic flexure. Peritoneal attachments are divided close to the bowel, retracting it to the right to expose the proper plane. The greater omentum is gradually separated from the colon using sharp dissection in the avascular plane near the bowel wall, moving from left to right.
The surgeon removes the colon from the snare retractor and positions it into the left abdomen, marking the transition to phase three of the operation.

The assistant grasps the dorsal mesenteric attachments of the terminal ileum and retracts them anteriorly. The surgeon incises the retroperitoneal attachments of the terminal ileum medial to the cecum, carrying the dissection cephalad and to the left toward the inferior edge of the duodenum. The ileal and right colonic mesentery are freed from the retroperitoneum by blunt dissection, creating a tunnel over the duodenum. The ileocolic artery and right colic artery are identified on the dorsal surface of the mesentery.

The surgeon creates windows on either side of the vessels on the ventral surface of the mesentery, highlighting the ileocolic artery. The ileocolic pedicle is then ligated and divided using an endoscopic vascular stapler.

The surgeon highlights the duodenum and pancreas as they proceed with dissection up the mesentery towards the middle colic vessels. The middle colic vessels are identified, cleared of surrounding tissue, and ligated using large clips or an endoscopic linear stapler.

The surgeon divides the remaining transverse mesocolon after controlling the middle colic vessels, providing a view into the lesser sac. The omentum is detached from the proximal transverse colon, approached from left to right.

The transverse colon is positioned downward as the surgeon divides the remaining omental attachments. The right colon and hepatic flexure are completely detached from the retroperitoneum, beginning at the cecum. With most mobilization performed dorsally, only minor lateral and posterior attachments remain. The hepatic flexure is lowered, and the colon is moved into the pelvis for extraction.

The surgeon converts the suprapubic cannula site to a small incision for specimen extraction. The ileum is divided, and an O polypropylene suture is placed around its cut edge. The anvil and center rod of the circular stapler are inserted into the ileum and secured with a purse string suture. The ileum is returned to the abdomen, which is then irrigated. The incision is closed, and pneumoperitoneum is reestablished.

Under laparoscopic guidance, the surgeon uses a double stapling technique to form the ileo-rectal anastomosis. The ileal mesentery is visually inspected along its cut edge to ensure it is not twisted. After completing the anastomosis, the pelvis is filled with irrigation fluid, and the bowel is occluded proximally. The anastomosis is then checked for leaks by air insufflation of the rectum using a proctoscope.
